1:
"When thou goest out to battle against thine enemies, and seest horses, and chariots, and a people more than thou, be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt." The witnesses note the charge not to fear the stronger foe, for the LORD is with them — the crux of faith in God's presence against superior force. On "when thou goest out to battle... and seest horses, and chariots, and a people more than thou, be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee," interpreters expound the charge. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the courage of faith — "be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt": however strong the foe, Israel was not to fear, for God who redeemed them from Egypt was with them; the witnesses note the significance — faith in God's presence overcomes the fear of superior force (the horses, chariots, and greater numbers of the enemy not to be feared, because God is with his people); the lesson — that the presence of God is the ground of courage, not the strength of arms (); the truth that God's people need not fear the strongest foe, for one with God is a majority, "if God be for us, who can be against us?" Romans 8:31; "some trust in chariots, and some in horses: but we will remember the name of the LORD our God," Psalm 20:7; the figure — the courage of faith that rests on God's presence, "fear thou not; for I am with thee," Isaiah 41:10; the warning against the fear that looks to the enemy's strength rather than to God. The figure of the courage of faith in God's presence against the stronger foe.

On "when thou goest out to battle against thine enemies, and seest horses, and chariots, and a people more than thou, be not afraid of them," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the charge. Keil and Ellicott note the charge not to fear the stronger foe, the horses and chariots no ground of fear (the courage of faith, Psalm 20:7) — the charge (be not afraid).

On "for the LORD thy God is with thee, which brought thee up out of the land of Egypt," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the presence. Calvin and Gill note the ground of courage, God's presence with his people, the Redeemer from Egypt with them in battle (the past deliverance the ground of faith, Romans 8:31; Isaiah 41:10) — the figure (the battle the LORD's, the victory by his presence); the providence (the charge, the priest encouraging the people to follow, vv.2-4). The verse displays the charge not to fear the stronger foe, for the LORD is with them.

The witnesses agree the substance: however strong the foe, Israel was not to fear, for God who redeemed them was with them (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of faith in God's presence: be not afraid — the courage that rests not on arms but on the presence of God (Psalm 20:7; Romans 8:31), the battle the LORD's and the victory by his presence (1 Samuel 17:47; Isaiah 41:10). The verse displays the faith-in-God's-presence crux. The charge not to fear the stronger foe, for the LORD is with them, displays the crux: be not afraid of them: for the LORD thy God is with thee (Psalm 20:7; Romans 8:31; 1 Samuel 17:47) — the courage of faith that rests on God's presence and not on arms. The verse displays the priest's charge to the people before battle (vv.2-4).

10:
"When thou comest nigh unto a city to fight against it, then proclaim peace unto it." The witnesses note the command to offer peace to a city before attacking it — the crux of the priority of peace and mercy even in war. On "when thou comest nigh unto a city to fight against it, then proclaim peace unto it," interpreters expound the command. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the offer of peace — "when thou comest nigh unto a city to fight against it, then proclaim peace unto it": before besieging a city, terms of peace were first to be offered; the witnesses note the significance — the priority of peace, even in war the offer of mercy first (the city not to be attacked until peace had been proclaimed and refused, the chance of submission without bloodshed); the lesson — that even in lawful war, mercy and peace were to be sought before the sword (the offer of peace the first resort, war the last; the humanity of the law that spared bloodshed where it could); the truth that God's people were to be peaceable even in war, seeking peace first, "if it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men," Romans 12:18; the figure — the gospel pattern of peace offered before judgment, mercy held out before the sword falls, "first... desireth conditions of peace," Luke 14:32; the truth that God himself offers peace and reconciliation before judgment, the long-suffering that calls to submission, "we pray you in Christ's stead, be ye reconciled to God," 2 Corinthians 5:20; the figure of the offer of peace that precedes the judgment, the mercy that goes before wrath; the warning that to refuse the offered peace is to bring the war on oneself. The figure of the priority of peace and mercy, the offer made before the sword.

On "when thou comest nigh unto a city to fight against it," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the approach. Keil and Ellicott note that before besieging a distant city, the offer was to be made (the war restrained by mercy) — the approach (nigh the city).

On "then proclaim peace unto it," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the offer of peace. The verse displays the command to offer peace to a city before attacking it.

The verse displays the priority-of-peace crux. The command to offer peace to a city before attacking it displays the crux: when thou comest nigh unto a city to fight against it, then proclaim peace unto it (Romans 12:18; Luke 14:32; 2 Corinthians 5:20) — peace and mercy to be offered before the sword even in lawful war, the city given the chance of submission, a figure of the gospel offer of peace and reconciliation that precedes the judgment. The verse displays the terms if the city accept or refuse the peace (vv.11-15).

The verse holds together the terms of accepted peace.

19:
